title: Kaitiakitanga i te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Ngāti Kahungunu's relationship with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college lands. They will learn about the traditional practices of monitoring the stream's health, the pūrākau of the taniwha who protects these waters, and contemporary challenges facing this significant waterway.
learningObjectives: Whakamahi i ngā kupu Māori e hāngai ana ki te kaitiakitanga me te taiao (use Māori vocabulary related to guardianship and the environment)
Whakamārama i te pūrākau o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku me te hononga ki ngā tikanga a Ngāti Kahungunu (explain the story of Mangakōtukutuku Stream and its connection to Ngāti Kahungunu protocols)
Waihanga i tētahi rautaki reo Māori hei whakapakari i te ora o te awa (create a Māori language strategy to enhance the wellbeing of the stream)
Whiriwhiri kōrero ki te hunga kaikawe i ngā kōrero tawhito mō te awa (engage in dialogue with knowledge holders about the stream's history)

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero - Walk and Talk
activities.description: Guided walk along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua, learning traditional names for native plants (harakeke, tī kōuka), birds (pūkeko, tūī), and water quality indicators while practicing directional language and environmental vocabulary

activities.title: Te Pūrākau o te Taniwha Kaitiaki
activities.description: Interactive storytelling session about the taniwha protector of the stream, followed by group creation of modern retellings using digital storytelling tools with te reo Māori narration and subtitles

activities.title: He Wānanga Ingoa Wai - Water Naming Workshop
activities.description: Research traditional Māori names for different water states (wai māori, wai kōpiro, wai tai) and create new descriptive names in te reo for specific sections of our stream based on water quality observations

activities.title: Ngā Taonga Tuku Iho - Cultural Mapping
activities.description: Create bilingual maps identifying significant sites along the stream, recording traditional place names, historical sites, and current environmental features using both Māori and English annotations

activities.title: Te Rautaki Kaitiaki - Guardianship Strategy
activities.description: Develop and present a te reo Māori action plan for stream restoration, incorporating traditional knowledge, modern science, and community engagement strategies to present to local iwi representatives

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hone Smith (local knowledge holder for Mangakōtukutuku area)
'Te Awa Atlas' digital resource from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
Traditional Māori water quality assessment methods from 'Te Mana o te Wai' guidelines
Māori plant identification app 'Ngā Tipu Whakaoranga' from Manaaki Whenua Landcare Research

tasks: Create a 5-minute digital story in te reo Māori about the stream's significance
Develop a bilingual water quality monitoring guide using traditional Māori indicators
Present a restoration proposal to kaumātua using appropriate cultural protocols

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Whakarongo (Listening) - comprehend spoken Māori about environmental topics
Te Reo Māori: Kōrero (Speaking) - present information about kaitiakitanga using appropriate language
Tikanga-ā-Iwi: Understand how cultural practices shape relationships with environments
Pāngarau: Use measurement and data collection in cultural context
